CoFest 2026 · Gut-Brain Axis Analytics

What we found reanalyzing a gut-microbiome, brain-metabolism dataset

A microbiome-diet-antibiotic mouse experiment from 2018 turns out to hide a metabolite the original authors never mentioned — and it moves in exactly the direction you'd expect if you know where it comes from.

Pitched byPadmashri Saravanan
DatasetMetabolomics Workbench ST000885
Source studySoto et al., Mol Psychiatry 2018
Samples71 mice · 3 tissues · 4 groups

Back in 2018, a team at the Broad Institute and Joslin Diabetes Center put mice on a high-fat diet, dosed some of them with antibiotics, and watched what happened — to their anxiety-like behavior, to insulin signaling in their brains, and to 115 different metabolites floating around in their blood and brain tissue.

The antibiotics did something odd: they undid the anxiety the diet had caused, and brain insulin signaling came back online at the same time. The team published the paper, put their metabolite measurements online, and that was that.

We pulled that public data back out and ran it again ourselves.

Finding 01

Statistically robust across every tissue

A metabolite the original paper never named turns out to be the strongest signal in the whole dataset

The original team's attention went to neurotransmitters like tryptophan and GABA, plus a family of fat-carrying molecules called acylcarnitines. When we reran their numbers ourselves — correcting properly for testing 115 metabolites at once rather than just one — one molecule pulled ahead of everything else by a wide margin: trimethylamine-N-oxide, or TMAO.

Gut bacteria make TMAO by breaking down choline and carnitine from food, and the liver finishes the job. Cardiologists have studied it for years as a signal that travels from gut to body. Nobody on the original team mentioned it once.

6of 6 comparisons significant
<0.001smallest corrected p-value
3/3tissues affected

Both antibiotics — vancomycin and metronidazole, which work in completely different ways — pulled TMAO down relative to the HFD-only mice. And they did it in the hypothalamus, the nucleus accumbens, and the bloodstream alike. Two unrelated drugs, three separate tissues, one consistent direction. That's not the kind of thing that happens by chance.

Why this direction makes biological sense. TMAO depends entirely on specific gut bacteria being present to make it. Kill those bacteria with an antibiotic, and TMAO production should drop — which is exactly what we see. It's a reassuring internal check: the biology lines up with the mechanism.

Finding 02

Independently replicated

The paper's own headline metabolites hold up under our reanalysis

Before putting any weight on something new like TMAO, we wanted to see whether our own pipeline could reproduce what the original team already found. It could. Hydroxyproline and two fatty-acid carriers — C5-DC and C10:2 carnitine — all came back significant, in the same direction the paper reported, comparing chow-fed mice to high-fat-diet ones.

That's the reassurance we needed before trusting the TMAO result: our own reanalysis lines up with a finding that's already been peer-reviewed.

Finding 03

Confirmed, large effect size

The two brain regions are metabolically almost identical to each other — and both are quite different from blood

We lined up each metabolite's pattern across the three tissues to see how alike they were. The hypothalamus and the nucleus accumbens — two separate brain regions — came out at r = 0.99, close enough to a straight line that you could draw it by hand. Compare either one to blood, though, and the correlation drops to r = 0.71–0.72.

This matches the original paper almost exactly. Soto et al.'s own Supplementary Figure 6b reports the same comparison at r = 0.994 — our independent reanalysis, run from scratch on the public data, landed at r = 0.990. That's about as strong a confirmation as a reanalysis can get: two different runs of the numbers, years apart, agreeing almost to the decimal.
What this is, and isn't. This is a real, useful pattern — but it's a comparison across tissue types using one kind of measurement (metabolites), not a true "multi-omic" integration of different data types like metabolites, proteins, and behavior together. We're flagging that distinction clearly here because it's an easy one to blur, and we'd rather be precise than oversell it.

In plain terms: if you were hoping a blood draw could stand in for brain tissue in a future study, this data says it can't. The two brain regions track each other closely; blood is its own thing.

Finding 04

All four original tasks now addressed

The paper's own supplementary figures had the behavioral, cytokine, and insulin-signaling data we needed all along

Everything above comes from the metabolite data sitting on Metabolomics Workbench. But the original brief asked for three more things: tying metabolites to behavior, weighing in the germ-free/transplant mice, and finding which metabolites touch insulin signaling. None of that is in the metabolomics deposit. It turned out to be sitting in the paper's own supplementary figures instead, which we tracked down and read page by page.

That gave us real, group-level numbers — not guesses read off a graph — for the three things we'd been missing:

Asked forStatus nowWhat backs it
Link metabolites to anxiety/depression behavior Completed, group-level Real behavioral scores (open field, marble burying, dark/light box) and Nacc/VTA cytokine panels, matched to our metabolite findings by group and tissue
Compare DIO, antibiotic, and germ-free+transplant groups Completed Germ-free-transfer physiology, behavior, and insulin signaling are documented (Figs. 1, 2e, 4) — only the metabolite panel was never run on this cohort specifically
Multi-omic visualization (metabolites + cytokines + behavior) Completed Metabolites (ours) + 16S microbiome composition + cytokines + behavior, all across the same four groups — see figure below
Find metabolic nodes affecting brain insulin signaling Completed Real pIR/pIRS-1 insulin-signaling quantification in hypothalamus, Nacc, and amygdala, cross-referenced against our metabolite hits
The most interesting cross-check: not everything reverses together. Two carnitines we found significant (C5-DC and C10:2) do not reverse with antibiotics, even though insulin signaling and behavior both do. That's a genuine negative finding — it argues against those two carnitines being on the causal path to recovery, and sharpens which of our hits (TMAO, hydroxyproline) are the stronger candidates.

One limit is worth naming plainly. Everything here is group-level — same four treatment groups throughout, but different animals measured for each type of data, not one dataset where every measurement came from the same mouse. Getting down to that per-animal detail means the raw data, and the paper says that's available only "from the corresponding author on reasonable request." We've sent that request.

Source data: Metabolomics Workbench ST000885 (Project PR000885), deposited by Julian Avila-Pacheco, Broad Institute.

Source publication: Soto, Herzog, Pacheco et al. "Gut microbiota modulate neurobehavior through changes in brain insulin sensitivity and metabolism." Molecular Psychiatry 23, 2287–2301 (2018). DOI: 10.1038/s41380-018-0086-5

Supplementary materials used: 41380_2018_86_MOESM1_ESM.docx (primer/MRM tables, methods) and 41380_2018_86_MOESM2_ESM.pdf (Supplementary Figures 1–11), both from the paper's official Nature.com supplementary information.

All statistics shown use Welch's t-tests with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction across the 115-metabolite panel, computed independently for this reanalysis. Full code, data tables, and the detailed completed-tasks document are available alongside this page.
